name: Subtraction with Regrouping (Borrowing)
whatIsIt: Sometimes when we subtract, the ones room doesn't have enough: in 52 − 27, we cannot take 7 ones from 2 ones. Regrouping rescues us: we UNBUNDLE one ten into 10 ones. Now the ones room has 12, the tens room has one less — and the subtraction works. Borrowing is just a bundle opening up.
whyNeeded: Change at a shop, runs still needed to win, pages left to read, days until a birthday — real life is full of take-away questions where the ones don't have enough. Regrouping makes subtraction work for ALL numbers, and it is the exact reverse of the carrying you already know.
realLifeUses: Shopping change: you give ₹52 for a ₹27 toy — how much back?
Cricket: chasing 52 runs and 27 are scored — how many still needed?
Reading: a 52-page book with 27 pages done — pages left?
Savings: ₹85 in the piggy bank, ₹47 spent on a gift — what remains?

vocabulary.meaning: Open 1 ten into 10 ones when the ones don't have enough.
The answer of a subtraction — how much is left or how far apart.
Untape a ten-rod back into 10 single cubes.
Answer + what you took away should give back the start.

story.title: Subbu the Squirrel Shares His Acorns
story.text: Subbu the squirrel has 52 acorns and must give 27 to his cousins before winter. 'I can't take 7 ones from just 2 ones!' he frets. 'Then UNBUNDLE a ten,' says Chatur the myna. 'Open one ten-bundle into 10 loose ones. Now you have 4 tens and 12 ones.' Subbu gives away 27 and counts what remains: 25 acorns. 'Borrowing is a trade, not a loss!'
story.extractedProblem: Subbu has 52 acorns and must give away 27. How many acorns are left?
story.answerInStory: 25 acorns — one ten was unbundled into 10 ones, making 4 tens and 12 ones, then 27 were given away.

visual.caption: Can't take 7 from 2! Unbundle one ten → 4 tens and 12 ones. Now: 12−7=5 ones, 4−2=2 tens. Answer: 25.

teachingGallery.title: Count up instead of borrowing
teachingGallery.note: Jump up from the small number to the big one — no borrowing needed.

examples.caption: Or borrow: open one ten into ten ones when the top ones are too few.

standardMethod.summary: Subtract the ones first. Not enough? Borrow 1 ten (it becomes 10 ones), then subtract place by place.
steps: Write the bigger number on top, places aligned: 52 over 27.
Ones: 2 − 7 won't go. Borrow: the 5 tens become 4, the 2 ones become 12.
Ones now: 12 − 7 = 5. Tens: 4 − 2 = 2.
Answer: 25. Check by adding back: 25 + 27 = 52 ✓

mentalMathMethod.name: Shopkeeper's count-up
mentalMathMethod.whenToUse: When finding change or a difference.
steps: Start at the smaller number.
Count up to the bigger number in easy jumps (to the next ten, then on).
Add the jumps — that is the difference.

alternateMethods.name: Blocks method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE what borrowing really is.
steps: Build 52: five rods, two cubes.
To remove 7 cubes, untape one rod into 10 cubes first.
Take away 7 cubes and 2 rods; count what remains.
alternateMethods.example: 52 − 27 with blocks: unbundle → 4 rods 12 cubes → remove → 2 rods 5 cubes = 25.

alternateMethods.name: Check-by-adding
steps: Take your answer and add back what you subtracted.
25 + 27 should return 52. It does ✓
If it doesn't, hunt for the missing borrow!
alternateMethods.example: If you got 35 for 52−27, then 35+27=62 ≠ 52 — the check catches it instantly.

workedExamples.problem: You pay ₹52 for a toy costing ₹27. How much change do you get?
steps: Set up: 52 − 27, places aligned.
Ones: 2 − 7 won't go → borrow. Tens 5→4, ones 2→12.
12 − 7 = 5 and 4 − 2 = 2 → change is ₹25.
Shopkeeper check: 27 + 25 = 52 ✓

workedExamples.problem: Ravi computed 52 − 27 = 35: 'Ones: 7−2=5. Tens: 5−2=3. Easy!' What sneaky flip did he make?
steps: In the ones he flipped the order — he did 7−2 instead of 2−7!
Subtraction is not flippable: the top number is what we HAVE.
2 − 7 won't go, so borrow first: 12 − 7 = 5 ones, and tens become 4 − 2 = 2.
True answer: 25. Check: 35 + 27 = 62 ≠ 52, so 35 was impossible.
workedExamples.answer: 25 — never flip; when the top is short, borrow.

trickPractice.intro: Instead of borrowing, count UP from the small number in easy jumps.

commonMistakes.mistake: Flipping the ones to avoid borrowing: doing 7−2 instead of 2−7 in 52−27 (getting 35).
commonMistakes.fix: The TOP number is what you have — you can't take more than it from thin air. When the top digit is smaller, that's your signal: unbundle a ten, like Subbu.

commonMistakes.mistake: Borrowing 10 ones but leaving the tens digit unchanged (52 − 27 = 35 by keeping 5 tens).
commonMistakes.fix: A borrow is a trade, not a gift! The opened rod LEFT the tens shelf: cross out the 5, write 4. One shelf gains only because the other gave.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing 52 − 7 with the 7 under the 5, subtracting from the wrong column.
commonMistakes.fix: Line up from the RIGHT: ones under ones, tens under tens. Squared paper keeps each digit in its lane.

easy.q: Subbu has 41 acorns and gives away 17. How many are left?
hintLadder: Ones: 1 − 7 won't go. What does Subbu do?

easy.q: In 52 − 27, why do we borrow before subtracting the ones?

options.label: Because 2 ones are not enough to give 7

easy.answer: Because 2 ones are not enough to give 7
hintLadder: Look at the top ones digit — how many do we HAVE?
Can a shelf with 2 singles hand out 7?
No — so a ten must be opened first.

medium.q: You pay ₹63 for a book costing ₹38. How many rupees change?
hintLadder: Ones: 3 − 8 won't go → borrow. Tens 6→5, ones 3→13.
Check by adding: your answer + 38 should be 63.

medium.q: Shopkeeper style — count UP to solve 52 − 27 without borrowing: 27→30→52. What is the difference?
medium.explain: 3 + 22 = 25 — counting up gives the same answer as borrowing!

challenge.q: Anu solved 74 − 28 and got 54. Her ones were right (14−8=6)… wait, she wrote 54! Which trade did she forget?

options.label: She borrowed 10 ones but never reduced the 7 tens to 6

challenge.answer: She borrowed 10 ones but never reduced the 7 tens to 6
hintLadder: The borrowed ten came FROM somewhere.
challenge.explain: 74 − 28 = 46. A borrow always costs the tens shelf one rod.

challenge.q: Piggy bank: ₹85 saved, ₹47 spent. How much is left? (Then check by adding!)
hintLadder: Ones: 5 − 7 won't go → borrow. Tens 8→7, ones 5→15.
Ones: 2 − 5 won't go → borrow. Tens 6→5, ones 2→12.

questions.q: When we borrow in subtraction, the ten we open…

options.label: Leaves the tens place — the tens digit goes down by 1
Is free — the tens digit stays the same

questions.answer: Leaves the tens place — the tens digit goes down by 1
hintLadder: Where did Subbu's 10 singles come from?
He snipped open a rod from the tens shelf.
A trade, not a gift: tens lose one, ones gain ten.

questions.q: A 90-page storybook, 36 pages read. How many pages are left?
hintLadder: Ones: 0 − 6 won't go → borrow. Tens 9→8, ones 0→10.

questions.q: The safest way to be SURE a subtraction is right:

options.label: Add the answer back to what you took away — you should get the start
Subtract the smaller digit from the bigger digit everywhere

questions.answer: Add the answer back to what you took away — you should get the start

questions.q: Chasing 71 runs; 45 scored so far. How many runs are still needed?
hintLadder: Ones: 1 − 5 won't go → borrow. Tens 7→6, ones 1→11.

teachBackPrompt: You are Subbu's shop assistant today! Explain to someone at home why we can't just flip '2 − 7' into '7 − 2', what really happens when a ten is unbundled, and show the shopkeeper's counting-up trick with 52 − 27.

revisionCard.summary: Ones too small? BORROW: open 1 ten into 10 ones (tens digit drops by 1 — a trade, not a gift!). Never flip the digits. Always check by adding back: 52−27=25 because 25+27=52 ✓. Shopkeeper shortcut: count UP (27→30→52 = 3+22 = 25).

gameMission.title: The Acorn Change Counter
gameMission.brief: Customers are queuing at Subbu's shop! Give perfect change every time — snip open ten-rods when the singles run short, and catch Ravi's sneaky digit-flips before an acorn goes missing.

realLifeProject: Play shop with a parent: they 'buy' something for ₹27 and pay with ₹52 (paper money is fine). Give change two ways — column borrowing AND counting up like a real shopkeeper. Which felt faster?

abacusMethod.name: Unbundle a ten into the ones
abacusMethod.whenToUse: To subtract with borrowing on the abacus.
steps: Try to take the ones away on the ones rod.
If not enough, UNBUNDLE one ten: remove a bead from the tens rod, add ten to the ones rod.
Now take away, and read the rods.

visual.caption: Unbundle one ten into ten ones, then take away.

vedicMethod.name: All from 9 and the last from 10 (Nikhilam)
steps: Subtract every digit from 9…
…and the LAST digit from 10.
Read off the answer — no borrowing needed.

rememberIt.hook: Can't subtract? Borrow ten from the next column
rememberIt.unpack: If the top digit is too small, take 1 from the column to the left (it becomes 10 more here). Work right to left.

studentQuestions.a: When the top digit is smaller, take 1 from the next column left - it adds 10 here so you can subtract.
